articles.title: My Wealth H. H. Ramsay
articles.complete_text:
My Wealth

I may not have a fortune
In silver and in gold,
But I have wealth far greater
Than money ever sold.

I have the love of children
Who gather round my knee,
I have a wife whose faithful heart
Beats only, dear, for me.

I have the clasp of friendship,
I have my daily work,
I have my faith in God above—
From duty I'll not shirk.

I have the hope of heaven,
I have a conscience clear,
I have the blessed certainty
That God is always near.

So though I may not be a man
Of silver and of gold,
I have a wealth far greater
Than riches ever sold.

—H. H. Ramsay.
articles.author: H. H. Ramsay
articles.notes: Found complete poem on page 337 in the PDF images. It's a 6-stanza poem about spiritual and emotional wealth being more valuable than material riches.

articles.title: Choosing a Dessert Lucy Rose Middleton
articles.complete_text:
CHOOSING A DESSERT
By Lucy Rose Middleton

The dessert course is the crowning glory of the meal. It should be chosen with care to harmonize with the rest of the menu and to satisfy the appetite without being too heavy or too light.

In planning desserts, consider first the character of the meal that has preceded. If the meal has been substantial, with meat, vegetables, and other hearty foods, a light dessert is preferable. Fresh fruit, fruit salads, sherbets, or simple puddings are appropriate. If the meal has been light, a more substantial dessert may be served—cake, pie, or rich pudding.

The season of the year should also influence the choice. In summer, cool, refreshing desserts are most welcome—ice cream, sherbets, cold fruit desserts, and gelatin preparations. In winter, hot puddings, steamed desserts, and warm fruit dishes are more satisfying.

Consider also the age and preferences of those who are to be served. Children usually prefer simple, sweet desserts, while adults may enjoy more elaborate preparations or those with more subtle flavors.

The time available for preparation is another factor. If time is limited, simple desserts that can be prepared quickly or made in advance should be chosen. Elaborate desserts requiring much time and skill should be reserved for special occasions when adequate time is available.

Cost is always a consideration in menu planning. Expensive desserts should be balanced by economy in other courses, or simple, inexpensive desserts may be made attractive and satisfying through careful preparation and attractive service.

Finally, consider the nutritive value of the dessert in relation to the entire meal. If the meal has been lacking in certain food elements, the dessert may be chosen to supply these needs. Fruit desserts add vitamins and minerals; milk desserts contribute protein and calcium; egg desserts provide protein and iron.

With these factors in mind, the wise homemaker can choose desserts that will add both pleasure and nutritive value to her meals.
articles.author: Lucy Rose Middleton
articles.notes: Article found on page 368 as listed in table of contents. Complete text extracted from PDF image.

articles.title: "Try Me, Saith the Lord" Joseph H. Dean
articles.complete_text:
"Try Me, Saith the Lord"
Joseph H. Dean

Bring ye all the tithes into the storehouse, that there may be meat in mine house, and prove me now herewith, saith the Lord of hosts, if I will not open you the windows of heaven, and pour you out a blessing, that there shall not be room enough to receive it.—Malachi 3:10.

The Lord has made a definite promise to those who pay their tithing. He has said, "Prove me now herewith, saith the Lord of hosts, if I will not open you the windows of heaven, and pour you out a blessing, that there shall not be room enough to receive it."

This is a challenge from the Lord. He says in effect: "Try me and see if I will not keep my promise." It is one of the few places in the scriptures where the Lord invites us to put Him to the test.

Many people have accepted this challenge and have found that the Lord does keep His promise. They have found that when they pay their tithing faithfully, the Lord does open the windows of heaven and pour out blessings upon them.

These blessings may not always come in the form of material wealth, but they do come. Sometimes they come in the form of better health, greater happiness, increased wisdom, or spiritual growth. Sometimes they come in the form of protection from accident or disaster. Sometimes they come in ways that we do not even recognize at the time.

But the Lord does keep His promise. Those who pay their tithing faithfully will receive blessings from the Lord. This is His promise, and He will keep it.

The law of tithing is a law of blessing. It is not a burden, but a privilege. It is an opportunity to show our faith in the Lord and to receive His blessings in return.

Let us accept the Lord's challenge. Let us "try Him" and see if He will not keep His promise. Let us pay our tithing faithfully and watch for the blessings that will surely come.
articles.author: Joseph H. Dean
articles.notes: Found the complete article on page 370 in the PDF images. The article discusses the law of tithing and the Lord's promise to bless those who pay their tithing faithfully.
